# invariants

<!-- awf:edit current-state: from .awf/domains/parts/invariants/current-state.md -->
## Current state

An invariant is a current-state topic claim, not an ADR declaration. Inside a topic's `## Claims` section a claim heading is exactly `` ### `invariant: <slug>` ``, and its metadata block ends with a `Backing:` line: `Backing: test` requires a proof marker, while `Backing: unbacked` forbids one and instead carries a single nonempty `Verify:` line, a reasoned contract exempt from mechanical proof. A rule claim (`` ### `rule: <slug>` ``) is normative without any backing metadata. The full identity of every claim is `<domain>/<topic>:<slug>`, globally unique by construction, and an ADR creates, changes, or retires an invariant claim through its `## State changes` operations; the ADR corpus itself declares no invariants.

`awf check` validates the backing ledger from markers configured under `currentState.sources`. A source entry has a nonempty `globs` array of anchored path globs (matched against slash-separated repo-relative paths per ADR-0077, so `**/*.go` is any depth and `internal/**` scopes a subtree), a nonempty opening `marker` token, and an optional `close` token. Three marker forms are recognised, each opening its own comment line: `state: <domain>/<topic>:<slug>` narrows selection to that claim within an already-applicable topic; `invariant: <domain>/<topic>:<slug>` is the test proof of a `Backing: test` claim; and `touches-state: <domain>/<topic>:<slug> - <note>` records an advisory production site for either claim type and never backs. A relevance or touches marker must resolve inside the topic and domain intersection and never expands topic scope; a proof marker may live outside production topic scope only when it matches both a configured source and a `currentState.testGlobs` glob (`**/*_test.go` here, so backing means an executed test line). Enforcement is symmetric: a `Backing: test` claim with no proof marker in scope, a `Backing: unbacked` claim carrying a proof marker, and an unbacked claim missing its `Verify:` line each fail `awf check`; an unknown or out-of-scope marker fails, while a note-less `touches-state:` marker is a non-failing advisory.

Backing is a ledger, not a proof (ADR-0114). The scan is a textual line match: it strips leading whitespace and tests whether a line begins with the marker, with no awareness of whether the marker sits inside a test function or relates to any assertion. So the check confirms a proof marker for the claim exists in scope; it does not confirm the backing test actually asserts the invariant, and a marker over a trivial or non-asserting test still passes. Backing is therefore bookkeeping that a test is *declared* to back the claim (the ledger `awf check` polices tightly), not evidence the property holds. Confirming that a backing test asserts what it claims is the `code-reviewer`'s job, not the scanner's. This is the marker-scan counterpart of the coverage gate's limitation (see "Coverage is not verification" in the testing doc): the coverage floor proves a line runs, the marker scan proves a marker exists, and neither proves an assertion.

ADR-0121 widens the scan surface to prose: a `currentState.sources` entry may declare a `close:` token (`-->`, `*/`) stripped once from a matched marker line's end before tag parsing, so block-comment markers yield clean touches notes. awf's own config points a `<!-- awf:comment` marker at `.awf/**/parts/**/*.md` and `templates/**`, so parts and templates can carry `touches-state:` tags that `awf context` surfaces and the render strip keeps out of output. The standalone `awf invariants` report reads the same qualified topic claims, listing each invariant claim with its backing state and proof sites.
